title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Role of Public Libraries in the Digital Age

texts.A: The persistent nostalgia for physical libraries is obstructing our transition to a more efficient information economy. This sentimentality, however charming, has a very real financial cost that falls disproportionately on underfunded municipal budgets already stretched to breaking point. In an age where the sum of human knowledge is accessible via a smartphone, [37]maintaining vast, climate-controlled buildings to house paper books is an ecological and financial absurdity.[/37] The library of the future should not be a place, but a digital license—a subscription to the world's knowledge, available to every citizen on any device at any time. Funding should be redirected from physical maintenance, staffing, and real estate to the acquisition of universal e-book access for all citizens, regardless of their socioeconomic status. The equity argument traditionally used to defend physical libraries is better served by ensuring universal broadband access and subsidized device ownership. Funding the physical infrastructure of the nineteenth century is not a viable strategy for meeting the information needs of the twenty-first, and honest recognition of this fact is long overdue.
texts.B: Libraries have always been about more than just books; they are the 'third place'—essential social infrastructure that exists between the home and the workplace, providing a neutral, welcoming environment in which people from all backgrounds can coexist without the pressure to consume. [38]Eliminating physical libraries would disproportionately affect the most vulnerable members of society who rely on these spaces for internet access, job application help, and physical safety.[/38] For the elderly, the homeless, and the socially isolated, the library is often the only publicly accessible space where they are treated with dignity and offered practical assistance. [39]While the digital shift is real, it should complement, not replace, the physical stacks.[/39] [37]A library is a community anchor that a digital app can never replicate.[/37] The intangible social functions performed by these spaces—reducing isolation, fostering civic engagement, and providing a sense of collective belonging—are beyond the reach of any algorithm. This is a civic function that no digital platform has yet replicated, and one that communities will sorely miss if they allow it to disappear in pursuit of a misguided efficiency.
texts.C: The debate over digital versus physical books is a distraction from the real crisis: the erosion of the public square as a genuinely accessible civic institution. I agree that libraries are vital community hubs, but we must be honest about their changing usage patterns rather than defending a nostalgic model that no longer reflects reality. [38]In many urban centers, libraries are becoming de facto day centers for the homeless or quiet study halls for students, with book circulation plummeting.[/38] Rather than lamenting this shift, we need to lean into this evolution and design services around demonstrable community needs. [40]Libraries should be rebranded as 'Innovation Labs' or 'Community Workshops,' focusing on 3D printing, coding classes, and meeting spaces rather than quiet reading rooms.[/40] This transformation would secure political support, attract sustainable funding, and ensure these institutions remain genuinely relevant to the communities they were built to serve. Embracing this evolution honestly, rather than apologizing for it, will secure these institutions their rightful place in the civic landscape for decades to come.
texts.D: Critics of physical libraries often cite the efficiency of digital access, yet they consistently undervalue what algorithms cannot provide. [39]They overlook the importance of 'serendipitous discovery'—the act of finding a life-changing book by accident while browsing the shelves. This cognitive experience is lost in the algorithmic filter bubbles of the internet.[/39] Digital recommendation systems are designed to reinforce existing preferences, not to challenge them, and a culture shaped entirely by algorithmic curation will be an intellectually impoverished one. While I share the view that libraries must provide modern services like internet access, [40]this should not come at the expense of their primary role as custodians of printed knowledge.[/40] Physical books remain the most stable and democratic medium for long-term information storage—resistant to power outages, server failures, and corporate decisions to discontinue platforms. Libraries that abandon their printed collections will discover, too late, that they have surrendered their most irreplaceable asset.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the value of maintaining physical library buildings?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls physical buildings an 'absurdity' and wants them gone. Expert B argues they are 'essential social infrastructure' and 'community anchors' that must be preserved.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's opinion regarding the social role of libraries for marginalized groups?
questions.explanation: Expert B mentions libraries help 'the most vulnerable.' Expert C agrees, noting they have become 'de facto day centers' for the homeless and essential spaces for the community.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D about the unique benefits of physical books?
questions.explanation: Expert D emphasizes 'serendipitous discovery' and books as a 'stable medium.' Expert B supports this by stating that digital shifts should 'complement, not replace, the physical stacks.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on whether libraries should move away from their traditional focus on quiet reading and books?
questions.explanation: Expert C wants to move away from 'quiet reading rooms' toward 'Innovation Labs.' Expert D insists that new services 'should not come at the expense of their primary role as custodians of printed knowledge.'
